text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Mid-Treatment Assessment — Cycle 3 of 6 Paraplatin/PTX

ATTENDING: [Attending Gynecologic Medical Oncologist]
DEPARTMENT: Medical Oncology / Gynecologic Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Established Patient Follow-Up

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 47-year-old Ghanaian-American woman with high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, FIGO stage IIIC, presenting today for mid-treatment assessment following completion of cycle 3 of adjuvant carbo (AUC 5) / PTX (175 mg/m2, now reduced as discussed below) chemotherapy. She presents for toxicity review, laboratory evaluation, CA-125 surveillance, and treatment planning.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 47-year-old woman with germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC, 5382insC founder mutation) who was diagnosed with high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, FIGO stage IIIC (pT3c pN1 M0) approximately four months ago following presentation with progressive abdominal distension, early satiety, and a 6 kg unintentional weight loss. Her CA-125 at diagnosis was markedly elevated at 3,847 U/mL. She underwent primary debulking surgery (TAH-BSO, infracolic omentectomy, appendectomy, bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, peritoneal implant resection, and right hemidiaphragm stripping), achieving complete gross resection (R0). Final pathology confirmed pT3c pN1 HGSC with 4 of 18 pelvic lymph nodes and 1 of 9 para-aortic nodes positive, and serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma (STIC) of the right fimbriated end. Post-operative CA-125 at 6 weeks was 187 U/mL, still declining but not yet normalized. A pre-chemotherapy CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis showed no residual macroscopic disease.

She initiated adjuvant chemotherapy with carbo AUC 5 / PTX 175 mg/m2 IV every 21 days. Cycle 1 was tolerated without incident. During cycle 2, she experienced a Grade 2 carbo hypersensitivity reaction (facial flushing, urticaria, and mild chest tightness), which was managed by stopping the infusion, administering diphenhydramine 50 mg IV and hydrocortisone 100 mg IV, and successfully completing the dose using a 12-step desensitization protocol. Subsequent cycles have been administered with extended premedications and slow titration per our allergy desensitization protocol. Also during and after cycle 2, she developed Grade 2 alopecia with complete scalp hair loss, which was anticipated and for which she was fitted through our oncology wig program. She also developed Grade 1 peripheral sensory neuropathy in the fingertips bilaterally, which has been monitored closely.

Cycle 3 was administered according to the desensitization protocol for carbo and was completed without a repeat hypersensitivity reaction. She returns today at the mid-point of her 6-cycle regimen for assessment.

Currently, Ms. Osei-Bonsu reports the following symptoms:
- Fatigue: She describes persistent fatigue, which she rates as moderate — she is managing her household but has required naps on most days and has reduced her working hours. This is consistent with Grade 2 chemotherapy-related fatigue.
- Peripheral neuropathy: Tingling and numbness in the fingertips bilaterally, and she now notes early involvement of the toes. She can perform all fine motor tasks (writing, buttoning) but notes that fine manipulation is slightly slower. She does not describe pain. This is Grade 1-2 sensory neuropathy.
- Nausea: Mild nausea during the first 3-4 days of each cycle, well-controlled with ondansetron 8 mg TID and prochlorperazine PRN. No vomiting. No diarrhea.
- Alopecia: Complete scalp hair loss, as well as loss of eyebrows and eyelashes. She is using the wig provided and reports that the hair loss, while distressing, is manageable. She is engaged with the oncology support group which has been helpful.
- Appetite: Mildly reduced but she is maintaining adequate caloric intake. No significant weight change since initiating chemotherapy.
- Musculoskeletal: Mild diffuse myalgias and arthralgias for 3-4 days post-infusion (consistent with Taxol-related arthralgia/myalgia syndrome), resolving by day 5-6 of each cycle. She is using acetaminophen 1,000 mg every 6 hours as needed with partial relief.
- No febrile episodes. She checks her temperature at home per instructions and has had no fever. She has not required urgent care or emergency room visits since initiating chemotherapy.
- No shortness of breath, chest pain, or palpitations.
- No unusual bleeding or bruising.
- No mouth sores.
- Bowel function: Normal, no constipation or diarrhea. No blood in stool.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Fatigue as above. No fever, chills, or night sweats. Weight stable.
- HEENT: No oral mucositis. Mild dry eyes (likely related to loss of eyelashes). No vision changes.
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or edema.
- Respiratory: No dyspnea, cough, or wheezing.
- Gastrointestinal: Mild nausea, well-controlled. No vomiting, diarrhea, or constipation. No abdominal pain or distension.
- Genitourinary: No urinary symptoms. No vaginal discharge or bleeding (post-BSO status).
- Musculoskeletal: PTX-associated arthralgias/myalgias as above. No joint swelling.
- Neurological: Peripheral sensory neuropathy as above. No weakness, gait difficulties, or hearing changes.
- Skin: Alopecia as above. No rash, erythema, or desquamation. No nail changes noted yet.
- Hematologic: No bleeding, bruising, or petechiae. No lymphadenopathy noted by patient.
- Psychiatric: Patient describes ongoing anxiety about the cancer diagnosis and its implications, but reports that the support group has been very helpful. She continues to have difficult moments emotionally but overall is coping better than at the start of treatment.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vital signs: Temperature 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure 118/74 mmHg, heart rate 78 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16, SpO2 98% on room air. Weight: 62.8 kg (stable compared to pre-chemotherapy baseline of 64 kg, approximately 1.2 kg loss — within acceptable range, no concern for significant nutritional decline at this time).

General: Well-appearing woman, appears comfortable and in no acute distress. Alopecia evident (scalp, eyebrows, eyelashes). Wig in place.

Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Anicteric sclerae. Dry eyes (bilateral). Oral mucosa moist without ulceration, erythema, or thrush. No oral mucositis.

Neck: Supple. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy palpable. Thyroid non-enlarged.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheeze, rhonchi, or crackles. No dullness to percussion.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No palpable masses or organomegaly. Post-laparotomy midline incision healing well, scar flat and without erythema or drainage. No ascites by physical examination (no fluid wave). Bowel sounds present in all four quadrants.

Extremities: No lower extremity edema. No calf tenderness. Peripheral pulses intact bilaterally.

Neurological: Alert and oriented ×4. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Motor strength 5/5 in all extremities. Reduced light touch sensation in fingertips bilaterally (consistent with Grade 1-2 sensory neuropathy); toe sensation mildly reduced at distal tips bilaterally. Vibration sense preserved at the ankles. Proprioception intact. Gait normal. Romberg negative.

Skin: Diffuse alopecia as noted. No rash or petechiae. Nail beds without hyperpigmentation or Beau's lines noted at this time.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (TODAY):

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 4.1 K/uL (normal range 4.0-11.0)
- ANC: 2.2 K/uL (adequate; no neutropenia)
- Hemoglobin: 10.8 g/dL (Grade 1 anemia; baseline pre-treatment was 11.4 g/dL)
- MCV: 89 fL (normocytic)
- Platelets: 198 K/uL (within normal limits)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 139 mEq/L
- Potassium: 3.7 mEq/L (lower end of normal; patient advised to maintain potassium-rich diet)
- Chloride: 102 mEq/L
- Bicarbonate: 24 mEq/L
- BUN: 14 mg/dL
- Creatinine: 0.84 mg/dL (eGFR >60 mL/min/1.73m2; renal function preserved — important for continued Paraplatin dosing at AUC 5)
- Glucose: 94 mg/dL
- Total bilirubin: 0.6 mg/dL
- AST: 28 U/L
- ALT: 22 U/L
- Alkaline phosphatase: 74 U/L
- Total protein: 6.8 g/dL
- Albumin: 3.8 g/dL (slight decrease from baseline 4.1 g/dL; adequate nutritional status)

Tumor Markers:
- CA-125: 42 U/mL
  This is a meaningful decline from 187 U/mL pre-chemotherapy (an approximately 78% decrease). The CA-125 is approaching but has not yet normalized (normal <35 U/mL). This trajectory is highly encouraging and consistent with an ongoing, deep chemosensitive response to platinum/taxane therapy. Given that the prior surgical specimen demonstrated germline BRCA1 with somatic LOH and a high HRD score (68), the robust marker response is not surprising.

Magnesium: 1.8 mg/dL (low-normal; repleted with magnesium oxide 400 mg PO daily going forward to help mitigate neuropathy risk).

---

IMAGING:
No new imaging obtained at this visit. Restaging CT imaging is deferred until after cycle 6 completion per standard institutional protocol for HGSC adjuvant chemotherapy. The most recent imaging (post-operative pre-treatment CT, obtained approximately 6 weeks post-surgery) showed no evidence of residual disease. We do not anticipate interval imaging between cycles in the absence of clinical concern for progression.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 47-year-old woman with high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, FIGO stage IIIC (pT3c pN1, germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant, high HRD score 68), status post R0 primary debulking surgery, now mid-way through adjuvant carbo/Taxol chemotherapy. She is at her cycle 3 mid-treatment checkpoint.

1. DISEASE STATUS / BIOCHEMICAL RESPONSE:
CA-125 has dropped from 187 U/mL (pre-chemotherapy) to 42 U/mL today — a robust downward trajectory, though not yet fully normalized. This is a very favorable biochemical response at the mid-treatment assessment and strongly suggests ongoing platinum sensitivity, which is expected given her BRCA1 status and high HRD positivity. We anticipate further normalization by post-cycle 6. Patient and family educated that CA-125 normalization is the goal and that we are on the right trajectory.

2. CHEMOTHERAPY — CYCLE 4 PLANNING:
Cycle 4 of Paraplatin AUC 5 / PTX 175 mg/m2 is tentatively planned for the next scheduled infusion date (day 21 from cycle 3). carbo will again be administered using the full 12-step desensitization protocol with extended premedications (dexamethasone 20 mg IV, diphenhydramine 50 mg IV, famotidine 20 mg IV, methylprednisolone 125 mg IV added per allergy protocol). The renal function remains adequate for Paraplatin dosing. The ANC and platelet count today are satisfactory for proceeding.

Regarding PTX dose: At this time, the neuropathy remains Grade 1-2 and is not yet causing significant functional limitation (patient can perform all instrumental ADLs). I will continue current PTX dosing for cycle 4 with close reassessment. Patient instructed to return immediately if neuropathy worsens or becomes painful between now and cycle 4. If neuropathy progresses to Grade 2 with functional impairment or Grade 3 prior to or during cycle 4/5, I will reduce PTX to 135 mg/m2 (approximately 20% dose reduction). The importance of completing 6 cycles to achieve adequate cumulative platinum exposure is emphasized. Cumulative pacli toxicity will be monitored vigilantly.

3. CHEMOTHERAPY-RELATED TOXICITIES:
   a. Grade 1-2 Peripheral Sensory Neuropathy: Fingers and toes bilaterally. Current management: patient using physical therapy tips (water temperature monitoring, protective gloves). Magnesium supplementation initiated today. No duloxetine started yet; will consider if neuropathy progresses. Patient counseled to avoid activities with high injury risk (working with very hot liquids, etc.) and to wear non-slip footwear. Threshold for dose reduction: progression to Grade 2 affecting ADLs or Grade 3.
   b. Grade 2 Fatigue: Attributed to cumulative chemotherapy effect and mild anemia. Hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL — Grade 1 anemia, not a transfusion indication at this level. No erythropoiesis-stimulating agent given curative intent. Recommendations: energy conservation, regular light activity (walking, yoga as tolerated), adequate hydration, and iron-rich diet. Rest as needed.
   c. Grade 1 Anemia (Hgb 10.8): Normocytic, consistent with chemotherapy-related anemia. Continue to monitor. Transfusion threshold if symptomatic or Hgb <7-8 g/dL.
   d. carbo hypersensitivity: Well-managed with desensitization protocol. No reaction during cycle 3. Continue full protocol for all remaining cycles.
   e. Chemotherapy-related nausea: Grade 1, well-controlled. Continue ondansetron 8 mg TID days 1-3 post-infusion, prochlorperazine 10 mg PO q6h PRN breakthrough nausea. Lorazepam 0.5 mg PO PRN for anticipatory nausea if needed.
   f. Arthralgias/myalgias: Taxol-associated. Managing with acetaminophen. No NSAIDs or aspirin (thrombocytopenia risk not currently an issue, but platelet trends monitored). If acetaminophen inadequate, may consider short course of dexamethasone 4 mg PO days 2-3 post-infusion.
   g. Alopecia: Grade 2 (complete). Expected to begin recovering approximately 2-3 months after completion of chemotherapy. Patient using wig and has connected with wig program. Support group ongoing.
   h. No oral mucositis, no significant weight loss, no febrile neutropenia to date. Pegfilgrastim has been administered per protocol after each cycle and will continue.

4. SUPPORTIVE CARE:
   - Continue pegfilgrastim 6 mg subcutaneously 24 hours after each chemotherapy cycle.
   - Antiemetics: ondansetron 8 mg TID × 3 days post-infusion; prochlorperazine 10 mg q6h PRN breakthrough.
   - Magnesium oxide 400 mg PO daily for neuropathy prophylaxis and electrolyte repletion.
   - Acetaminophen 1,000 mg q6h PRN for PTX arthralgia/myalgia.
   - Multivitamin daily (no high-dose antioxidants during chemotherapy).
   - Hydration encouraged: ≥2 liters of fluids daily.
   - VTE: Patient completed 28-day post-surgical enoxaparin prophylaxis. Now on low-risk oral contraception-free post-surgical status. No current anticoagulation indicated. VTE risk discussed; patient advised to maintain ambulation and activity.
   - Psychosocial: Patient engaging with oncology support group with reported benefit. Oncology social work involved. No formal psychiatric referral at this time, but patient aware of referral availability.

5. UPCOMING PLAN:
   - Cycle 4 carbo/pacli: next scheduled infusion at day 21 from cycle 3, pending CBC clearance (CBC to be drawn day of infusion, proceeding if ANC ≥1.5, platelets ≥100).
   - Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and CA-125 measurement: planned approximately 3-4 weeks after cycle 6 completion.
   - Post-chemotherapy maintenance therapy: Patient has germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant with somatic LOH and achieved R0 resection. She is a strong candidate for PARP inhibitor maintenance therapy. Options to be discussed in detail at the post-cycle 6 visit: Zejula (Zejula) vs. ola (Lynparza). Both have FDA approval in this setting; we will discuss individualized dosing, side effect profiles, and patient preference at that time.
   - Follow-up appointment: In-clinic visit scheduled for day of cycle 4 infusion and CBC check on day 21.
   - Patient instructed to call the oncology triage line immediately for: fever ≥100.4°F, rigors, new chest pain, significant worsening of neuropathy (loss of hand/foot function), fall, inability to take oral fluids, or any other concerns.

6. PSYCHOSOCIAL:
Ms. Osei-Bonsu continues to demonstrate remarkable resilience. She has expressed ongoing anxiety related to her diagnosis and the implications for her children (she has already been connected with genetic counseling for her family members given her germline BRCA1 status). She finds the oncology support group helpful and meaningful. She is encouraged to continue. She was reassured that the ongoing response to chemotherapy, the excellent result from surgery, and her BRCA1 status — while meaning she carries a germline risk — also means she has a biologically predictable, PARP inhibitor-sensitive tumor that we know how to treat effectively.

Time spent with patient: approximately 45 minutes.
Patient verbalized understanding of the plan. All questions answered. Return precautions reviewed.

Electronically signed by:
[Attending Gynecologic Medical Oncologist]
[Department of Medical Oncology / Gynecologic Oncology]
